It will have been noticed by our readers
that we have kept ourselves pretty much
in the background on the Central Factory
question in this island, at least of late.—
We supposed the question to be fully un
derstood from the discussion it has been
subjected to at an earlier stage in this
paper, and from the papers and documents
accessible to the public.
We have accordingly considered it super
fiuous to go further into the question in
its local application and have confined our
selves to such information as we have
gathered from other sources which we
have given at some length, perhaps too
much so for the taste of some It would
appear however that there are certain
points not yet understood by every body,
one of which is the pecuniary relation
,bet‘weer? the Cane-grower and the Factory.
This being the case we need not apologize
for repeating what we said as far back
as the 9th of July last year. viz. that
the payment for canes to the grower
is to be the value (medium price)
of 53 Ibs. of Muscovado Sugar for every
100 bs. of canes with division of profits be
tween the G?‘ozvcr and the Factory equally, af
ter a certan percentage has Zone to the Fac
tory, and @ Reserve Fund has been paid up—
(l4 p. C1). This point has been explain
ed by Governor Garde in the Council in
the following words: ¢ There is one
point of special interest to cane-growers to
which I would call attention, viz. the
conditions for the delivery of canes; as
stated in the documents signed by the respective
partes, it was understood that canes are to be
delivered on condition of the parties recewving
the value of 6% lbs. Muscovado sugar at mar
ket price for every 100 lbs. of canes delivered,
and the net profits after 14 ps Ct. to be equal
ly dwided between the deliverers of canes and
the sharcholders of the Factories, however not
before a Reserve Fund had been established.
Thas will be altered so that the Reserve Fund
will be established out of the 14 p. Ct., and
the division of the net proceeds will commence
at m/('(f./;f/‘ the (/c/(.c'c/'t;l'.s' Qf'(v‘/lnc‘.s‘.”
These words are so plain that they can
hardly require any further explanation.
The prineiple of dividing profits is
copied from Martinique and is to be pre
ferred to the other modes of payment
prevalent there, viz: 5 Ibs.,, 5% Ibs.
or G llbs. without share of profit to
the grower. But itis more advantage
ous to the planter than that of L’
Usine du Simon in Martinique, which
offers as great advantages as any Fac
tory there. The following is ore of
the scctions of the statutes of that
Factory: “The Reserve Fund consti
tuted, all tbe anuual revenues exceeding
11 p. Ct. as interest to the shareholders,
3p Ct. for amgrtization and U 0 of the
surplus to the administrator, shall be ap
plied one-half to the sharcholders and one
halt to the deliverers of canes in propor
tion to the sums aceruing to each oue of
these latter for canes delivered for the
year.” .
To the foregoing we would beg to add
that we have understood that Mr. Ha
gemann has come here for the purpose of
settling this vital question of the estab
lishment of Factories, and all those who
are vot systematically opposed to the idea
itself<=and we do vot think there are any
of that category here——will no doubt em
brace the opportunity of seeking informa
tion from one in a position to give it with
authority. We are confident that this will
be the case, and shall therefore abstain
from going into detail about other ad
vantages offered, for instance that -of a
large loan on cheap interest, which will be
profitable to sharcholders and cane growers
alike, under the provisions of the principle
of division of profits between the former
and the latter.
Sunday evening about 6 o'clock the
dead body of a Coolie was found on the
public road in the neighbourhood of the
Istate Barren Spot. Oun the left side of
the dead man’s breast there was a wound,
which is supposed to have caused his
death. The corpse proved to be that of
the Coolie Rampaul from the Estate
Diamond & Ruby. Suspicions were at
first directed against some Coolie labour
ers, but the Police investigations have
since taken another direction which, it
is to be hoped, will lead to the detection
of the peérpetrator, and we doubt not that
such exemplary punishment will be inflict
ed as would tend to put a stop to the fur
ther outbursts of the ‘brutish amd murder
ous propensities, which have of Jate been
too prevalent among our hitherto compara
tively quiet and peaceful rural labouring
class. .

Booker Washington's Work,
Tho Springfield, Mass., Republican contains an
interesting account of tlio last annual conference
at the Tuskegco Institute. For ton years this
conference has boon held at Tuskegco, Ala.,
and Bookor T. Washington has been its leading
spirit. Tho institute is really a part of tho edu
cational work which has made Mr. Washington
tho most conspicuous living member of his race.
Moro than two thousand negroes, representing
farmers and artisans as well as teachers and stu
dents, wore assomblcd in tho chapel and the time
was devoted to short speeches, caoh participant
presenting in a few words his own experience or
his views upon the subjects under discussion.
Mr. Washington reviewed the progress of the
colored raco during tho last half century and pre
dicted oven greater improvement during tho next
fifty years. Ho warned his hearers against self
indulgence and going into debt; and urged upon
them tho importance of saving their money and
buying a homo.
Reports wore presontod from other similar
sooioties; a Texan woman described a society
formed in her state, with twenty-five hundred
members, which had purchased fifty thousand
acres of land and was doing much to help poor
men to seouro homes.
Tho resolutions adopted by the conference
represent so well tho purpose and spirit of tho
gathering, as expressed by tho speeches, that they
are given in full:
1. "Wo have reached tho 10th annual session of
tho Tuskegco negr.o conference. During all tho years
since tho conferonce was started, we have clung
steadily to ita original purpose, namely, to encourage
tho buying of land, getting rid of the one-room cabin
and tho abuso of tho mortgage system, the raising
of food supplies, building better school houses, the
lengthening of tho school term and tho securing of
bettor teachers and preachers, tho doing away with
sectarian prejudice, tho improvement of the moral
condition of tho masses and the encouragement of
friendly relations between the races. In all these
particulars wo aro convinced from careful investiga
tion that substantial progress is constantly being
made by the masses throughout tho South.
3. Wo would urge our people not to become dis
couraged while tho raco is passing from what was
largely a political basis to an economic one, as a
foundation for citizenship.
8. Wo urge, since the country school is the back
bone of tho intelligence of tho masses, that no effort
bo spared to increase its efficacy. Any injury to the
country schools brings discontent to the people and
leads them to move to tho cities.
4. Statistics show that crime, as a rule, is not
committed by those who have received literary, moral
and industrial training.
5. Regardless of .how others may act, we urge
upon our raco a rigid observance of the law of the
land, and that we bear in mind that lawlessness
begets crime and hardens and deadens not only the
conscience of the law-breaker, but also the conscience
of the community.
6. Tho rapid riso in tho price of land throughout
the South makes it doubly important that we do not
delay in buying homes, and tho increased demand for
skilled workmen of every kind makes it necessary
that a larger proportion of our young people prepare
themselves for trades and domestic employment
before they are crowded out of these occupations.
7. Community and county fairs, as well as local
conferences and farmers' institutes, should be organ
ized as rapidly and widely as possible.
8. Wo call tho attention of our women, especial
ly, to the wealth there is for them in tho garden tho
cow the pi and the poultry-yard. '
f. We note with pleasure that landlords are
The Commoner.
building better houses for their tenants. We feel
sure that all such improvements are a paying invest
ment from every point of view.
Mr. Washington's work is worthy of all com
mendation. His school, with thirteen hundred
students" and nearly a hundred teachers, is a splen
did monument to his own energy, ability and
lofty purpose, as well as proof that the negro's
hope lies in his intellectual and moral devel
opment. Kace prejudice and race pride are such that no
white man could do the work that Mr. Washing
ion is doing. No white man could so secure the
confidence of the negros or win their affection,
arid without confidence and affection little can be
done. It would be fortunate for the country if
Mr. Washington's work at Tuskegee could be
duplicated in othe southern states. Tho white
people of the south have thus far furnished
almost all tho money used in the education of the
negros and they have, of course, an immediate
and powerful interest in the uplifting of the race.
But the work which is being done by Bookor
Washington ought to appeal strongly to the
philanthropists of the North, many of whom
have been quick to condemn the southern whites,
but slow to aid them in solving the tremendous
problem which was thrown upon them by eman
cipation. W
Prof. Ross on Cheap Labor.
The dismissal of Prof. Ross from Stanford
University occurred before The Commoner- en
tered upon its journalistic career and its readers
may not have seen the speech which led Mrs. Stan
ford to demand his resignation.
She had been more or less hostile to him since
1896 because of his opposition to the gold stand
ard, and this hostility had been increased by the
i-Tofessor's advocacy of the municipal ownership
of municipal franchises; but the speech on coolie
labor was the last grievance, and, added to the
others, caused her to protest against his retention
as a member of the faculty.
It has been the custom of western railroads to
employ Asiatic labor, and Mrs. Stanford prob
ably felt that Mr. Ross' remarks were a reflection
upon her husband's business methods.
Of course, there is no excuse for her conduct
unless the university which she has so liberally
endowed was intended not for an 'institution of
learning, but as a literary bureau for the vindica
tion of Mr. Stanford's financial operations, but
aside from their bearing on the university contro
versy, Prof. Ross' words are valuable because
they present the Chinese question from his point
of view.
The speech was delivered in the city of San
Francisco and the following extract is taken
from the report given by Organized Labor:
But what American labor objects to is exposure
to competition with a cheaper man. The coolie can
not outdo him, but ho can underlive him. He can
not produce more, but he can consume less. The
Oriental can elbow the American to one side in the
common occupations because ho has fewer wants. To
lot him go on, to let the American be driven by coolie
competition, to check the American birth-rate in or
der that the Japanese birth-rate shall not be checked
to let an opportunity for one American boy be occu
pied by three Orientals so that the American will not
add that boy to his family, is to reverse the I rren
of progress, to commit race suicide, Everything w,
call progress lias helped to develop man vrho can pro
duce much and can consume much; it has abhorred
the cheap man. It has favored and fostered not tho
man of crude palate, of tough stomach, of low organ
ization, of few wants and of little intelligence and
energy, but tho superior man.
Starting with tho Oriental peoples of Egypt and
Babylonia, civilization has in 3,000 years swept around
the globe, insisting on better, finer and brainier men
as it went, and now by tho waters of the Pacific it is
face to face with peoples like the Chinese and Hindoos
and the Japanese, who are nearest to.reproducing the
economic conditions of those ancient peoples which
rocked the cradle of civilization.
Shall we allow the process of uplifting tho com
mon man to be defeated by this confrontation? Shall
wo suffer the work already done to be nullified?
Shall we look idly upon this inverted competition of
the cheap man and the dear man, the low-grade man
and the high-grade man, and allow tho survival of
the unfittest to proceed unchecked?
A policy of restriction on immigration is not, un
der such circumstances, unfair or predatory or blame
worthy. Remember, we do not assail tho Oriental
peoples. We do not propose to treat tho Orient as
the powers are treating China. We are not grObing
lands or seizing ports, or extorting railway contracts,
or forcing trade at the cannon's mouth. Wo enter
tain no emnity and cherish no designs against the
trans-pacific peoples. We hope they will prosper and
elevate their working people by the means vre have
used. But we are absolutely determined that Cali
fornia, the latest and loveliest seat of the Aryan
race, shall not become, if wo can help it, tho theater
of such a stern wolfish struggle for existence as pre
vails throughout the Orient.

SEMI-WEEKLY MAUI NEWS, TUESDAY, FEBRUARY 21, 1922.
PAGE THREE
Philippine Sugar for
- Export is Estimated
MANILA, P. I., Jan. 10 (Associat
ed Press Mall) The production of
sugar for export from the Philippines
this season is estimated at 200,000
tons of centrifugal and 100,000 tons
of muscavado, by the Sugar, News.
R. Renton Hind, editor of the Sugar
News and general manager of one of
the largest sugar mills In the islands,
thinks that many of the sugar mills
will make a profit this season, despite
the low price of sugar.
"If centrals have cane to run them
to full capacity throughout the mill
ing season, they will make fair profits
this year at the current price of
sugar, 3 1-2 cents a pound", said Mr.
Hind. "We are back to normal In
sugar and high prices can not be ex
pected. There Is a big crop in Cen
tral Luzon and I think It will prove
to be the , largest ever harvested.
Field wages have dropped from more
than fifty cents . a day last year to
forty cents and labor is abundant. In
the Island of Negros field wages are
now about thirty cents a day where
as much as seventy five cents a day
was paid last year. That means that
the next crop will be planted at far
less expense than was the crop now
being harvested."
Balance of Trade Swings
Back for Philippine Isles
MANILA, P. I., Dec. 20 (Associa
ted Press) For the first time in
many months the balance of trade of
the Philippine Islands during Novem
ber was In their favor. The total
trade for the month amounted to $13,
176,000 of which $8,032,311 was for
exports and $5,143,851 was for im
ports. According to the bureau of customs
the excess of exports over imports is
due both to the fact that the Philip
pines have bought less than usual and
to a better market for copra, sugar
and hemp, some of the leading pro
ducts of the country.
Sexes in Philippines
Are Nearly Equal
MANILA, P. I., Jan. 10 (Associat
ed Press Mall The" native male, and
female population of the Philippine
Islands Is almost equal in numbers,
there being only 134 more native wo
men than men, according to a bulle
tin issued by the census bureau and
just made public. The men number
4,715,485 and the women 4,715,619.
According to the census bureau
there are 2,896,336 bachelors and 2,
690,901 unmarried native women in
the Islands.
The total population of the Philip
pine Islands at the end of 1921 is estl
mated at 10,850,086 by the census bu
reau. The census which was taken in
1918 placed the total population of
the islands at 10,314,310, according to
corrected figure' Just published.

SYDNEY, S. S. W. Jan. 11 (Asso
ciated Press Mail) The question of
coolie labor for northern Australia,
' advocated by W. A. Horn, former
member of the South Australian legis
lature, has given rise to considerable
i discussion in favor and against the
proposition.
j Premier Barwell of South Australia
! has expressed himself as favoring the
project as the territory la "a tropical
j country and capable of producing rice,
: maize, sugar cane, coffee, cotton,
tobacco and other commercial pro
ducts."
"It is a pity that a country of such
wonderful resources should remain un
productive and unpeopled a veritable
white elephant," he continued. "If it
be true that only colored races can
develop the country, the question
arises as to whether they should be
brought into Australia as coolie labor
under indenture or whether delected
Asiatics should be permitted to settle
with full citizen rights. The second
seems to me to be the only proposi
tion worthy of consideration. Such a
country cannot remain unsettled and
unproductive forever. I believe that
It will 'be realized ultimately that it
can only be settled and developed ef
fectively by colored people."

took issue with Premier Barwell, char
acterizing his argument as dangerous
and unpatriotic propaganda. He de
clares that if such a policy were
adopted the whole continent would be

I century.

Queensland to promote white settle

Robbers Shot to Death
In Streets of Shanghai
SHANGHAI, Jan. 15 (Associated
Press Mail) Four robbers were bhot
to death at noon today on the Iiund,
the principal street, by order ol Gen
eral Ho Feng-lin, defense commission
er who is the high administrative of
ficer over Chinese territory in Shang
hai, as a measure to check robberies,
which have become exceedingly pre
valent In the two foreign concessions
of Shanghai and in surrounding terri
tory. On the Bund are erected the proud
est structures of the International Set
tlement. After crossing Avenue Ed
ward VII the Bund extends through
the French concession and thence in
to the thickly populated Chinese dis
tricts. Here the four robbers were
blindfolded and were shot by soldier's.
Prior to their execution, the four were
placed in rickshaws and ridden
around the city.

COOLIE COAT FOR TEAS.
Correspondem-e of the Associated Press.
PARIS CP).—The coolie coat, cos
tume of American beaches, has moved
into Paris salons as a sort of tea coat
and feminine smoking jacket.
Parisians make up in rich woolens
and gorgeous silver embroideries what
.originally was an humble little blue
cotton cloth coat worn by Chinese
workmen. The director of a fashion
house in the Place Yendonie wears a
1 coolie coat in his studio-office.

SCHEME TO DEVELOP
AREAS IN THE SOUTH
»
Proposal for Benefit of Rural Sec
tions Submitted by Delegates to
Reclamation Conference.
A scheme by which rural areas in
the South would lie developed by the
; Government without cost, by repav-
I ment of the entire appropriation to be
asked of Congress, was placed before
the Interior Department yesterday by
delegates to the Southern Reclamation
j Conference, which adjourned Thurs
| day. Appropriation of funds for recla
j motion and agricultural development
|in the. South would be made by Con
i press at the request of th« Interior
Department, the funds to be so safe
guarded that the national Treasury
will he reimbursed in full.
The conferee argued that such work
| as it contemplated is national in scope,
purpose and benefits, and that the in
! terior Department should undertake a
j particular project only after a thor
ough investigation of its feasibility. As
j a demonstration of the practicability of
the movement, the department was ex
■ j pected to undertake one trial project
under the scheme. Interior Depart
■ ment officials had hoped funds for the
j work might be obtained from private
i sources, with establishment of a
j “Southern foundation,” but delegates
jto the conference decided to go di
rectly to Congress for an appropri
j ation.
Opportunities and needs of the
j South make it an appropriate field for
\ demonstration that reclamation is a
■ | movement for better homes in country
! Mife centers, the conference declared.
i Under the plan adopted by the confer
cnee group settlements of farm home
owners would he established in the
South.
More apartments were built in Ham
lung. Germany, in life past seven
months than were constructed in the
1 entire year of 1926.

INDUSTRY FORMS
TEXTILE FORUM
Problems of Cotton Manufac
turers and Growers Will Be
Discussed at Meeting.
B* the Associated Press.
ATLANTIC CITY, N. J.. May 24
Pians lor organization of a ‘‘textile
forum’’ for discussion of problems in
the cotton industry were announced
today by Lincoln Baylies, president of
the National Association of Cotton
Manufacturers, at the opening of the
crganization’s annual convention.
The convention is being held jointly
with that of the American Cotton Man
ufacturers' Association.
The forum will be inaugurated Sep
tember 19 in Boston, Baylies said.
Meetings for groups of employes affect
ed by technical problems will be pro
vided as follows: Overseers of carding,
overseers of spinning, overseers of weav
ing, slashing and dressing, master me
chanics. overseers of cloth rooms,, dye
ing and bleaching agents, managers
and superintendents.
Cathrine Hooper, fashion authority in
the cotton industry, urged the delegates
to send cotton to Paris to have it
shown "in its proper sphere in the
fashion world.” She also advocated the
creation of a style research bureau as
a development of the present advisory
boards of the associations.

REFORM AIDED BY
WOMAN SUFFRAGE
Families Don't Quarrel About
Politics, Says Author.
KANSAS A DECIDED GAINER.
Declares That It Took Votes For Wom
en to Obtain Mothers' Pensions, Mini
mum Wages For Women and Tax
Exemption to Widowed Mother or
Single Woman.
William Allen White, editor of tlie
Emporia (Kan.) Gazette, author of "A
Certain Rich Mun,"vice president of the
American Short Ballot association and
member of the Progressive national
committee of 1912, states in a letter to
O. P. Connolly of Orange that it took
woman suffrage to secure mothers
pensions, a minimum wage law for
women and a law giving the right of
tax exemption as the bead of a house
to (he widowed mother or a single wo
man.
"Within three years, during which
women have had full suffrage in this
etate," Mr. White states, "every im
portant demand the women had asked
for and had been refused as unenfran
chlsed voters has been granted to the
franchised women.
"The women of Kansas have had suf
frage for thirty years in the towns and
cities upon all municipal matters. In
the election of town and city officers
and upon bond issues, franchises and
all matters that come before cities of
any class. My wife and mother have
been voters during that time, and 1
have lived In the state practically all
of those years. I have observed worn
«η suffrage from every angle.
"The danger of wrecking a home Is
not from without, but from within.
When the marriage of a man and a
woman ceases to be a partnership and
becomes a vassalage that marriage is
a failure even without divorce. The
extension of suffrage to women in Kan
•as has brought women up to a con
idderation in public uffalrx that ha.<
made them intelligent partners, given
them a wider vision and, I feel quite
sure, has mftde them more desirable
companions for their husbands, more
Intelligent mothers for their children
iind wiser keepers of their homes than
they could have been in a more re
•tricted life.
"It is not true that men and women
in the home vote alike. I know of η
hrflf dozen elections in recent years in
which either my wife or my mothei
and sometimes both my mother and
m.v wife have voted quite differently J
from wo. I know that I respect my I
wife and mother when they differ from I
me quite as much as when they agree I
with me. We do not quarrel over poll I
tics any more than we quarrel over 1
^^fcfjur taste In bookR In fur ,
ΐΒΓιΙ "
pmn^^qunrreI in any family, yet J
have known scores and hundred", of
women In différent campaigns who
have disagreed with their husbands
and have voted their disagreements
frankly."
How Strange.
There are no homes In suffrage states.
No children, happy, wise a.n<l good.
Men there no longer seek for mate»,
And women lose their womanhood.
I credit thla without debute,
And yet I ask, and oak In vain,
"Why no one in a suffrage state
Has moved to eh»n«c things back again
-Alice D. Killer.

IN A FRENZY OF EXCITEMENT.
♦ ι
Every Native In Town Make* a Mad
Dash For the Scene of the Conflagra
tion and Chaos Reigns Supreme. I
Even a Small Fire Is a Big Event.
A fir ο Is a great event In Japan. One
would think that, inasmuch as the
llimsy construction of the Japanese
houses and their packing together
nuke flree of almost dally occurrence—
In the cities, at any rate—Ores would
have lost a portion of their novelty
luring the ten or fifteen centuries Ja
pan bas regularly been burning down.
They have not, though. When the lire
hell begins to toll the whole popula
tion goes to the are.
1 was in a Jinriklsha in Yokohama
on my way to the railroad station
when 1 beard the clangor of a bell,
and the coolie who was drawing me
shouted: "KwaJII Kwajil"—or what
sounded like that—and displayed strong
evidences of breaking Into a gallop.
♦ "Sore wn nanigesuka?" I Inquired po
litely, remembering from my phraa·
book, "What is that?"
"Kwajl!" he shouted. "KwaJlP
And so It fell out tbat we went to
the fire—the coolie, the Jinriklsha and
myself—for I had no words to atop
him except a very uncertain "Tomarul"
And be did not choose to tomara.
The ce I plloped down one street
and I 1 and In tea minute· I
In every style of Japanese dress, and
all running backward and forward.
Not a man walked. All ran. They
were so interested and excited that I
thought they must be shareholders In
the shanties which were burning.
A man dashed wildly to one end of
the street elbowing people oat of his
way, stepping on them, pushing against
them, and then he stopped, emitted a
wild screech and dashed back again.
1 say on· man did that In reality a
thousand did it on each of the four
sides of the block in which the Are
was, unconcernedly and In a casual
and unhampered manner, demolishing
some small houses.
Other thousands—many of them—
ran this way a few steps and that way
a few steps, all hy-ahl-lng. Not a man
stood still except myself, and I soon
got away, for the Japanese, frenzied
with excitement, were bumping into
me from all sides.
Presently the firemen came. They
dashed In from all quarters of the city,
dragging little hose carte painted red
and flying white and red flags.
The hydrants In Yokohama are In
manholes below the surface of the
street. The firemen joined In the mill
ing throng. They ran back and forth,
too, and the spectators ran back and
forth after them. There wai much
shouting and gesticulation. Every
fireman, whether regular or TOlnnteer,
seemed to be a chief. They all gare
order· that nobody obeyed, like a toi·
pnteer fir· outfit in a country Tillage at
·"· ÉÊS
borne.
There were ten or fifteen minute· of
this running back and forth and shout
ing by the firemen; then some tiny
I streams of -water began to sprinkle on
the fire. Also, a few big engines be
gan to «quirt. Meantime the firemen
were paying no attention to the bouses
on fire, but were trying to keep other
bouses from burning. A few of tbem
tore slates from adjacent roofs and
cast tbem Indiscriminately Into the
crowd. Women with bundles of house
hold belongings straggled out of the
houses that might catch fire. More
thousands came. For four blocks each
way the streets were packed from wall
to wall with excited people, All run
ning back and forth and nil shouting.
I had to club my coolie with my can·
to make him start with me to the sta
tion, and bfe looked backward over his
shoulder all the way. 1 was depriving
htm of a real pleasure—taking some
thing out of his life by not allowing
him to stay and help put out that Ore.
It seems almost superfluous to say 1
missed my train or that the Jlnrlkisha
man, bowing and smiling, remarked,
"I am sorrow for you!" However, that
made me later in getting back that
night, and as I went up dark and de
serted Water street In Yokohama 1
heard music ahead of me—β kind of
music and tune that sounded familiar.
Presently I overtook the musician.
He was a Japanese youth, stumping
along on < tie wooden cjogs, wearing «
derby ha| end clad In α kimono, awl he
wus playing on a mouth organ "Shnll
We Gather at the River?" > Wherein
was Illustrated and exemplified the old
and tho new Japan.—Bamuel G. Blythe
In Saturday Evening Tost.
Three Greatest Lawyer·.
It would be rather difficult to pick
